text: AGIO-STRATI, an island on the Grecian archipelago, 6 leagues SW 1 W from Port Antonio in Lemnos; in N lat. 39° 31', E long. 25° 2'. There is a small v. on the western side. It is the ancient New.
AGLADO-CAMPO, a v. of the Morea, SW of Argos, on a small stream which flows into the gulf of Argos.
AGLASTERHAUSEN, a town of Baden, in the circle of the Lower Rhine, between Mosbach and Wiesenbach. Pop. 803.
AGLEKTE, an island belonging to the northern inspectorate of the W coast of Greenland, on which the station of Ritenbenk is situated.
AGLEMOUTES, an Esquimaux tribe, living under Russian protection, along the Nussegak.

AGLIA, or AGLIE, a town of Piedmont, situated upon a hill on the l. bank of the Malosina, 10 m. SW of Ivrea. Pop. 3,500. There is a fine palace or castle here, with an extensive library, and a good collection of paintings and antiquities.
AGLIATE, a v. of Italy, in Venetian Lombardy, on the Lambro, 15 m. N of Milan. Pop. 350.
AGLIN-DAGH, a mountain of Kurdistan, which branches-off from the great plateau on the SE of Lake Van.

AGLISH, a p. of Ireland, in co. Cork, intersected by the river Lee. Area 6,771 acres. Pop. 2,579, nearly all Catholics.-Also a p. in co. Kerry. Area 4,857 acres. Pop. 1,939, nearly all Catholics.—Also a p. in co. Waterford, on the E side of the Blackwater, containing the villages of Aglish and Villierstown. Area 6,856 acres. Pop. 3,783, of whom about 100 are Protestants.—Also a p. in co. Kilkenny, sometimes known as Aglish-Martin. Area 1,343 acres. Pop. 440.
AGLISH-CLOGHANE, a p. of Ireland, in co. Tipperary. Area, 5,898 acres. Pop. 1,938.
AGLISH-CORMICK, or LISCORMICK, a p. of Ireland, in co. Limerick. Area 1,141 acres. Pop. 738.
AGLISH-DRINAGH, or BALLINORANE, a p. of Ireland, in co. Cork. Area 3,310 acres. Pop. 1,026,
AGLOUEN, a v. of Eastern Prussia, in the prov. of Königsberg, circle of Memel. Pop. 53.

AGLY, or GLY, a small river of France, which rises in the dep. of l'Aude, and, entering the dep. of Pyrénées-Orientales, passes Saint-Paul, Estagel, and Rivesaltes, and flows into the Mediterranean, 9 m. NE of Perpignan, after a course of about 50 m. from NW to SE, of which about 35 m. are navigable during the winter-floods.
AGMONDESHAM. See AMERSHAM.
AGNA, a river of Lombardy, which flows in a S course, through the prov. of Brescia, to the Chiesa, which it joins near Vobanno.

AGNADELLO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. of Lodi, and district of Pandino, 12 m. N of Lodi. Pop. 1,600. On the 14th of May, 1509, Louis of France obtained a decisive victory over the Venetians under Pitigliano and D'Alviano, in the neighbourhood of this town.
AGNAM, a town of Senegambia, in the country of the Futatoras, of which it was formerly the capital, in N lat. 15° 15′, W long. 12° 49′.

AGNAN (SAINT), a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Saône-et-Loire. Pop. 1,627.—Also a hamlet in the dep. of Nièvre, sometimes known as Sr. A. DE Cosne. Pop. 1,000.—Also a com. and v. in the dep. of Drôme, sometimes distinguished as Sr. A. EN VERcors. Pop. 1,254.
AGNANA, a town of Spain, in Biscay, 15 m. W by S of Vittoria.
AGNANA. See MONTAGNANA.

AGNANO (Lago D'), a lake of Naples in the Terra di Lavoro, at the foot of the southern descent of the hill of the Camaldoli, 8 m. to the E of Lake Avernus. Eustace is inclined to identify it with the celebrated fish-pond of Lucullus; but Breislak, and some other naturalists, are of opinion that it is of later origin, and has been formed by volcanic action probably during the great eruption of the Solfatara in 1198. The water is dark-coloured, but not stagnant, although it shows no free current. At the SE edge of the lake, a small emission of carbonic acid gas occasions the phenomena of the well-known 'Grotto del Cane.' The lake is not mentioned by classic writers; but appears in the middle ages under the name Lacus Anclanus. Carter describes it as "a small, muddy, reedy pool, encircled by a high brim of hills;" Eustace, as "a fine expanse of water of a circular form, about 2 m. in circumference, yet deriving its greatest beauty from the verdure that borders its margin, and from the noble hills that rise around it and crown its basin."
AGNANO-DI-VAL-D'AMBRA, a v. of Tuscany, 20 m. NE of Arezzo. Pop. 426. An ancient abbey of Camaldolenses which existed here was suppressed in 1811.
AGNANO-NEL-PIAN-DI-PISA, a v. of Tuscany, at the foot of Monte Pisano, 4 m. SE of Bagnia-San-Giuliano. Pop. 469. There are some hot springs in the neighbourhood.
AGNANT (SAINT), a canton, com., and v. of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inférieure, 16 m. NE of Marennes. Pop. of the cant. 6,718; of the com. 1,113.
AGNAZZO, a small town of Naples, in the Terra di Bari, SE of Monopoli. Pop. 350.

AGNEAUX, a com. of France, in the dep. of La Manche. Pop. 934. AGNES (Sr.)—pronounced St. Ann's—a p. and sea-port town on the Bristol channel, in Cornwall, 9 m. NW of Truro. Area of p. 8,660 acres. Pop. in 1801, 4,061; in 1841, 7,757, chiefly miners, the neighbourhood, being a rich tin and copper mining district. The harbour can only be entered at high water by vessels of 100 tons.—St. Agnes Beacon, a hill in the neighbourhood, in 50° 18' N lat. and 5° 13' W long., has an alt. of 621 ft.—Also the southernmost of the inhabited Scilly islands, in N. lat. 49° 54', and 6° 21' W long. Area 390 acres. Pop. in 1841, 243. It is the best cultivated of the whole group. There is a lighthouse on the highest point of the island, with a revolving light, 138 ft. above high water-mark.
AGNETE, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Oise. Pop. 1,483.
AGNETENDORF, a vill. of Prussia, in the prov. of Silesia, circle of Hirschberg. Pop. 700.
AGNETTEN, or St. Agotha, a town of Austria, in Transylvania, 11 m. NW of Schenk on the Hartbach river. In the neighbourhood is the Vesszod, a lake containing a floating island.
AGNEW'S HILL, a conspicuous height in co. Antrim, Ireland. It is about 5 m. W by S of Larne; and has an alt. of 1,558 ft. above sea-level.
AGNIELLES-EN-BEAUCHENE, a v. of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Alpes, 16 m. W of Gap. Pop. 261.
AGNO, av. of Switzerland, in the cant. of Tessino, on a stream of the same name-sometimes also called the Vedeggio or Bedagio-at the point where it flows into Lake Lugano. Pop. 726. AGO

AGNO, or Anio, a river of Lombardy, which, rising to the W of Schio, flows in a SSE direction to Lonigo, fetches a circuit SE by E towards Este, and joins the Adige, on the l. bank, 15 m. below Este, at Rotta Sabadina.—Also a river of Naples, which, rising on the western border of the Principato Ultra, flows westward, passing Acerra on the r. bank, then turns NW, and sweeping round towards WSW, falls into the sea at a point 7 m. SE of the mouth of the Volturno. It sends off a branch near its mouth to Lake Patria.

AGNONA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo Citra, on a branch of the Trigno, 20 m. NNE of Isernia. Pop. 7,000. There are extensive copper works here. A. is supposed to mark the site of the ancient Aquilonia.
AGNUNI, a small port of Sicily, 10 m. S of Catania. Corn, oil, and rice, chiefly the produce of the fertile plain of Catania, are largely shipped here.
AGNY, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais. Pop. 1,003.
AGOA, a term of frequent occurrence in Portuguese and Brazilian topography, signifying 'water.' It is the same as the word Agua in Spanish topography.
AGOABRANCA, a v. of Brazil, in the prov. of Piahy, near the source of the Pirahim, in 10°34' S lat.
AGOADA (Point), the northern extremity of the bay of Goa, on the Malabar coast, at the mouth of the Mandova river, in N lat. 15° 30'.
AGOA DA SAUDE, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, falling into the Tocantins, on the l. bank, in about 5° S lat., after an ENE course of 120 m.

AGOA DE PAO, a v. on the S coast of the island of St. Michael in the Azores, between Alagoa on the W, and Villa Franca on the E. The Serra da A.— once a volcano and known as the Monte Volcamrises on the N of the v. to the height of 3,060 ft. above sea-level.—Hunt.
AGOA-DE-PIES, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, 18 m. N of Beja.
AGOAFRIA, a v. of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, 90 m. N of Bahia. Tobacco, mandioca, and sugar are cultivated in the neighbourhood.

AGOAPEHY, or AGUAPEHI, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Sao Paulo, descending from the Sierra de Ibotacatu, and flowing, in a NW course, to the Parana, which it joins on the r. bank.—There is another river of the same name in the prov. of Matto Grosso, which, rising on the eastern side of the Sierra de Agoapehy-which divides the waters flowing NW to the Guapore from those flowing SE to the Paraguay—runs in an easterly course towards the Jauru, which it joins on the r. bank.

AGOAQUENTE, or AGOAQUENTES, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Goyaz, near the junction of the Maranhao with the Tocantins, in S lat. 13° 58', W long. 49° 22'. The name, signifying 'warm water,' is derived from "a large and deep lake of brackish, fetid, and warm water" in the neighbourhood [Henderson]. A lump of gold weighing 43 lbs. was found in the arraial of A. during the early workings.
AGOA-REVEZ, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Tras-os-Montes, 20 m. WNW of Moncorvo.
AGOAS-BELLAS, a town of Portugal, in Estremadura, 10 m. NE of Thomar.
AGOAS-DAIRADAS, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Matto Grosso, flowing into the Parana, on the r. bank, in 22° S lat.
AGOAS-DE-MOURA, a town of Portugal, in Estremadura, 13 m. ENE of Setubal, on the r. bank of the Marateca.
AGOASUJA, an arraial of Brazil, on the r. bank of the Arazuahy, in the prov. of Minas Geraes, 25 m. NE of Bom Successo.
AGOAVERDE, a v. of Brazil, in the prov. of Piahy, on the r. bank of the river of the same name, and near its source.
AGOE, an islet of Sweden, in the gulf of Bothnia, off the coast of Gefleborg; in 61° 32' N lat., E long. 17° 35′.
AGOF, a Galla town of Abyssinia, in the prov. of Angote; in about 11° 8' N. lat., 39° 32' E. long.

AGOGNA, a river of Piedmont, which rises to the E of Lake Orta, and flows in a SSE course past Borgomanero, and a little to the W of Novara and of Mortara, and joins the Po, by several branches, on the l. bank, 12 m. E of Valenza.

AGOLINITZA, or AGULENITZA, a decayed town of the Morea, in the dep. of Triphylia, 5 m. SSE of Pyrgos. It stands on the side of a pointed ridge, which is separated by a narrow plain from a large lagune known as the A. lake or Ruféa fishery, extending southwards to Khaiaffa, and commands an extensive prospect of the Ionian sea, and the windings of the Alpheus or Rufea.—Leake.—Emerson.
AGOMI, a river of Siberia, rising in a lake on the NW declivity of the Stannovoi mountains, and flowing, by a NW course, to the Utchur, a head-branch of the Aldan.
AGOMSKA, a large island in James's bay or the southern part of Hudson's bay, in N lat. 53°, W long. 81° 5,' 50 m. NNE of Fort Albany.
AGON, a small port of France, in the dep. of La Manche, 6 m. W of Coutances, at the mouth of the Sienne. dogne. Pop. 1,719.
AGONCILLO, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Soria, 6 m. ESE of Logrono, on the r. bank of the Leza, near its junction with the Ebro. Lat. 42°25' N.
AGOONA. See AGUNA.
AGORA, a town of Abessinia, in the prov. of Tigré, 35 m. W of Antálo.
AGORDO, a town of Lombardy, in the district and 15 m. NW of Belluno. Pop. 1,825. In the neighbourhood are copper-mines said to be the richest in Italy.

AGOSTA, or LAGOSTA, an island in the Adriatic, off the coast of Dalmatia; in N lat. 42° 45', E long. 16° 52'. At the NW end is a small harbour. Off the E end is the islet Lagostini. Some Phænician antiquities are said to exist on this island.

AGOSTA, or Augusta, a town on a small isthmus on the SE coast of Sicily, in the prov. and 12 m. N of Syracuse; in N lat. 37° 14', E long. 15° 14'. A. was founded in the 13th cent., and was a place of considerable importance until devastated by a fearful earthquake in 1693, which destroyed nearly one-third of the inhabitants, and precipitated its light-tower into the sea. Though rebuilt on a regular plan it has never attained its former opulence. It exports salt made in salines to the N and W of A., and sardines, oil, honey, and wine. Pop. 10,000. A. is well-fortified, having a glacis or covered way towards the sea, and a fortress on the isthmus which unites the town with the main. In the environs are the celebrated caves of Timpa.—Smyth.
AGOSTINHO, or St. AUGUSTINE (CAPE), a bold rugged granitic promontory, on the coast of Brazil, 17 m. S from the city of Pernambuco; in S lat. 8° 21', W long. 34° 57'.
AGOSTINHO (SAN), a military post on the river Jucu, in the Brazilian prov. of Espiritu Santo. There is a settlement here formed by emigrants from the Azores.

AGOUT, a river of France, rising in the Cévennes, in the dep. of Hérault, traversing the dep. of Tarn, in which it passes Castres and Lavaur, and joining AGR Abessinia, which receives its name from the people inhabiting it. It extends from the r. bank of the Zingini, which separates it from Damot, towards the western frontiers of Abessinia. Its surface is rugged, and exhibits numerous traces of volcanic agency. It is in the hands of the Agows, who are divided, in this district, into seven tribes or clans. The slave-population are Shánkala negroes.—Beke.

AGOWS, or Agaus, an Abessinian tribe of that family which Ruppell characterises as being of the Caucasian type, and which embraces the greater part of the population of the mountains of Samen, and of the plains around Lake Tzana, as well as the Faláshas. One tribe of Agows lives on the W border of Abessínia, in the prov. of Agowmider. Another branch is located along the western bank of the Tacazze, from the mountains of Lasta to the plain of Tembien. They are generally shorter and stouter in their persons than the other Abessinians. Their language is harsh and guttural, and entirely different from the Abessinian; and the dialect spoken by the Agows in the prov. of Avergale is different from that of the Agows to the westward of Matscha. The people of Agowmider told Dr. Beke that their original country was Lasta, where there are still Agow tribes remaining. Dr. B. thinks it not improbable that the Falashas and Agows, or Agawis—as he calls them—are "the remains of the early inhabitants of Abessinia, who, in the course of ages, were displaced by more recent settlers from the opposite shores of the Red sea, or by invaders from the South." [Geog. Journ., vol. xiv, p. 10.] They profess the Christian religion; but, according to Bruce, some of them adhere to the religion of their ancestors, and worship the Nile.

AGRA, one of the fifteen subahs, vice-royalties, or grand divisions, into which the Mogul empire, under Ackbar, was divided in the 16th century. In the Ayeen Akbery it is thus described: "It is situated in the second climate. In length from Ghatimpoor,

AGONAI, a com. of France, in the dep. of Dor- which confines it on the side of Allahabad, to Pulwul, the boundary towards Dehly, it measures 175 koss [1 koss = 2 m. 5 furl.]; and its breadth is from Kinoje to Chundery in Malwah. It is bounded on the E by Ghatimpoor; on the N by the river Ganges; on the S lies Chundery; and Pulwul confines it on the W." It was divided into 13 sircars or counties; which again were subdivided into 203 pergunnahs or hundreds. The revenue was estimated at 1,61,56,257 rupees, or £1,615,625; and the military force at 50,600 cavalry, 577,570 infantry, and 221 elephants. When Ackbar made the city of Agra his capital, it became the leading province of his empire: but in the successive convulsions by which the once magnificent Mogul empire was dismembered after the death of Aurungzebe, this prov., with that of Delhi, was alternately ravaged by Jats, Sikhs, Mahrattas, and other predatory tribes, until, in the language of Rennel, writing towards the close of last century, having been the seat of continual wars for nearly 50 years, they were reduced to "the most wretched state that can be conceived," "almost depopulated, and most of the lands of course lying waste." The capture of the city of Agra by the British arms, and the subsequent treaty signed on the 30th of December 1803, transferred the whole territory between the Ganges and the Jumna, with considerable districts beyond the latter river, including the two Mogul capitals of Delhi and Agra, to English dominion; and an arrangement, under the new charter-act of 1833, has erected the North-west provinces of India into a new government, or lieutenancy rather, under the governor of Bengal, of which the seat of immediate or local administration is fixed at Agra. We shall first describe, in general terms, the extent and divisions of the Agra or North-west Lieutenancy; and then successively notice the province, the zillah, and the city of Agra.
AGRA LIEUTENANCY, or the North-West Pro-

VINCES OF BENGAL, embraces 24 collectorates or districts: viz.- Square Miles. Population. Acquired. 1. Benares, . * . 1 丁川區 A STATE OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE These districts form a territory stretching on the NW to the frontiers of Lahore and the Himalaya; bounded on the N by the king of Oude's territories and the Nepaulese dominions, into the hill-districts of which the authority of the lieutenant-governor of A. extends; on the E and SE they border on the Bahar provinces of Bengal; from the SE round to NW they are skirted by the rajah of Berar's territories, by Bhopal, Bundelkund, and the Rajputana territories.

AGRA (THE PROVINCE OF), is bounded on the N by Delhi; on the E by Oude and Allahabad; on the S by Malwah; and on the W by Ajmere; measuring about 250 m. in length, by 180 m. in breadth. The principal portion of this prov. lies in the plains of the Jamna and the Ganges; and the richest portion of it is that which comprises the alluvial plains lying between these two rivers, and known by way of eminence as the Doab or country of 'the Two rivers.' Towards the N and E, the country is flat and open; to the S of the Chumbul, and along the W frontier, it is better wooded, and more diversified by hill and dale.—The climate is temperate in the elevated districts; but the hot winds to which Central India is subject are felt here with peculiar severity. From November to February the general temperature is cold, especially during the night, and the weather serene and dry.—Millet, wheat, and barley form the principal grain-crops. Rice is little cultivated owing to the deficiency of water. A considerable quantity of pulse is raised as a winter-crop; and mustard and linseed are extensively grown for oil. The Doab produces sugar of excellent quality, cotton, and indigo. No minerals are wrought; but copper is said to exist here. The principal stone is a species of red sandstone of which the cities of Delhi and Agra are built. Marble exists in the neighbourhood of Futtehpore. Cattle of an inferior kind are reared in great numbers; but the horses are considered superior to those of Bengal. Game and fish are abundant.—The principal article of manufacture is coarse cotton-cloth. The pop. has been estimated at 6,000,000, the great mass of whom are Hindus. The Mahommedans are next in number, but bear a smaller proportion to the Hindus, in this the ancient seat of their Indian rule, than they do in the more remote districts of Bengal. The natives are a handsomer and more robust race than the Bengalese. Hindustani is the vernacular language; but Persian is spoken by the higher classes; and there are some traces of the earlier language of the country in a dialect called the Bruj, now nearly extinct, which is spoken in the neighbourhood of A. -The chief towns besides Agra, the capital, are Alvar, Muttra or Mathura, Etawah, Calpi, Narwar, Kanoje, and Furukabad. The zillah or district of A. has a computed area of 3,500 sq. m. according to Martin, but of 4,500 sq. m. according to Hamilton, of which not more than onehalf was under cultivation in 1813. AGR prov. of the same name, and one of the keys of Westthe Táj-Mahal, erected by Shah-Jehan in honour of his sultana Nurjehan. It is situated on the banks of the Jumna, about 3 m. from the city, and enclosed within an area laid out as a garden, with fruit-trees, walks, and avenues, which is kept in excellent order by the British authorities. The surrounding country is a wild plain, broken into deep sandy ravines,—the picture of rudeness and desolation. The building itself is raised on an elevated terrace of white and yellow marble, having at its angles four tall minarets of the same material. It contains a central hall, about as large as the interior of the Radcliffe library at Oxford, in which are the tombs of the emperor and empress. Round this hall are a number of small apartments. The walls, screens, and tombs are covered with flowers and inscriptions in mosaic. "The whole, whether seen inside or out, looks as if the scaffolding had not long been cleared away, and it was just fresh from the hands of the architect." [Sketches of India.]—A. carries on active commerce with the eastern districts of Hindostan. In 1813 its revenue dues amounted to 922,157 rupees; in 1818 they exceeded the produce of the preceding year by 130,000 rupees; and this increase has continued progressive. Its pop. was recently estimated at 96,597, of whom 65,011 were Hindus, and 31,579 Mahommedans. The houses were returned at 29,788. Once "inhabited by people of all nations, and where are displayed the productions of every climate" [Ayeen Akbery, vol. ii., p. 40], A., though much fallen-off from its original splendour, is still a plan of wealth and importance. Bishop Heber, indeed, describes it as a decayed city, "with little to attract attention beyond the picturesque mixture of houses, balconies, projecting roofs, and groups of people in the Eastern dress, which is common to all Indian towns." Yet it is a place of great traffic; the lower part of every house is a shop, and the bustle displaying itself in the bazaar indicates much commercial activity. It is inhabited by many rich natives, both Mussulman and Hindu; and a writer in the Asiatic Journal [vol. i., 1833, is of opinion that should steam-navigation ever be introduced with effect upon the Ganges and Jumna, there is much probability that the seat of government will be at some time removed from Calcutta to Agra. Doubtless the execution of the Great Trunk line of railway—which has been projected from Calcutta to Allahabad; thence, according to one scheme, from Cawnpore by Shekabad to Agra; and thence along the r. bank of the Jumna to Delhi-would give a great stimulus to A., and enable it to maintain its pre-eminence among the cities of Upper India.- There is a college at A. for the purposes of general education. In 1843, being the 20th year of its existence, this institution was attended by 495 students, of whom 281 were studying English; 322 Hindostani; and 177 Persian. Of that number 371 were Hindus by birth; 100 Mahommedans; and 24 Christians. [Statist. Journal, vol. viii., p. 119.] - A., originally a mere village, owed its imperial rank to Sakundar Lodi, who, in the 16th cent., made it the capital of his dominions, under the name of Badulghur. It fell into the hands of Madhadji Sindhiah, and remained in the possession of the Mahrattas until 1803, when, after a short but animated resistance, it surrendered to the British. It is now the provincial seat of government. It is called by the Hindus, Parasu Rama; and is held by them in great veneration as the place of an avatar or incarnation of Vishnu. It is likewise celebrated as the birth-place of Abul Fayal, the prime minister of Akbar. The principal roads and dawk stations concentrating upon A. are those from Cawnpore, by Mynparge, westwards to Jeypur, and from Gwalior northwards to Delhi -The following table of distances from A., in British miles, was calculated by Major Rennell: Hardwur, Ajmere, Madras, by Elichpur, Bareilly, -, by Nagpur, 379 1,187 Benares, Masulipatam, Bombay, by Burhampur, 848 71 | Seringapatam, Etayeh, 1,020 | Surat, 680 Goa, 887 Golconda, 790 | Tatta, 80 | Visapur, 825 Gwalior,
AGRA, a v. in Sinde, 55 m. N of Sehwan, near the W bank of the Indus.

AGRA'-DA'GH, a "great and massive mountain" Chesney of Asiatic Turkey, in the Taurus chain, rising to the N of Marash and E of Bostan, and running in a direction nearly SW and NE. It consists of tertiary sandstone and limestone.
A'GRAFA, a district of Albania, on the W side of Mount Pindus, or the Agrafiotic range as it is sometimes called, close on the northern frontier of Greece. The v. of A. is 16 m. SSW of Fanári.
AGRAFUS, or AGRAFUSI, a small town and canton in the NW part of the island of Corfu.

AGRAKHAN, or AGRAGANSKI (GULF OF), an inlet of the Caspian, on the coast of Daghestan, having its opening between the mouth of the Terek on the NW and Cape Agrakhan on the SE, and extending in a SSW direction 25 m. Cape Agrakhan is in N lat. 43° 36', E long. 47° 50', 85 m. N of Derbend. A chain of islets runs out from it in a NNE direction. Of these the principal one is called Tchetchen or Chitcheena. A small river, bearing the same name as the gulf, discharges itself at its head; and on the eastern side of it, not far from the embouchure of the A., Peter the Great established a port in 1722, and erected a fort for its protection; but this establishment was destroyed in 1735.

AGRAM, a town of Austria, the capital of Croatia, near the l. bank of the Save, 35 m. SSW of Warasdin, in N lat. 45° 49'. Pop. reckoned in 1815 at 17,266, but stated by Möller, in 1840, at only 11,290. The ban or governor of Croatia, the bishop, the chancellor, and the military commandants of Croatia and Slavonia have their head-quarters in this city.—The surrounding district, forming the county of Agram, is watered by the Save, the Lonya, and the Krapina. It produces grain, tobacco, timber, and fruit. On a superficial area of 108 sq. m., it had a pop. of 183,968, in 1815.
AGRAMUNT, a town of Spain, in Catalonia, 25 m. ENE from Lerida, 12 m. NW from Cervera, on the Sio, a tributary of the Segre. Pop. 3,000.
AGRAPI'DHO-KORI, a v. of Greece, in the dep. ~ AGU of Elis, near the junction of the Ladon with the Peneus .- Leake.
AGREDA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Soria, 25 m. ENE of Soria. Pop. 3,000. It occupies an elevated site on the N side of the Sierra Moncayo.
AGRE'VE (Sr.), a small town of France, in the dep. of Ardêche, 23 m. WSW of Tournon.
AGRI. See ACRI.
AGRIA. See EGER.
AGRIB. See AGARIB.
AGRI-DAGH. See ARARAT.
AGRIGAN, or GRIGAN, one of the Ladrone islands in the North Pacific. It lies to the S of the island of Assumption; and appears to be a cluster of volcanicrocks.
AGRIGENTUM. See GIRGENTI.
AGRILIO, a town of Greece, on the S side of the bay of Livadostro, 13 m. NE of Corinth. dom of Dahomey; in N lat. 70° 7'.
AGRIS, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of La Charente. Pop. 1,389. twixt Tiflis and Ararat, 2 m. S of Sametzk. Alt. thick forests; the S side is without a tree.—Parrot.
AGTELEK, a town of Hungary, 6 m. E of Gömör. In the neighbourhood is the celebrated stalactite cave of Boradla.
AGUA (CAPE), a steep headland of Spain, on the coast of Murcia, between Cartagena and Cape Pálos, a league W of Escombrera islet.

AGUA (VOLCANO DE), a gigantic mountain in Guatemala, to the S of Antigua or Old Guatemala. It is of a conical figure of graceful outline, its base extending over nearly all the western part of the valley of Guatemala. Stephens estimates its alt. at 14,450 ft. above sea-level; but as the height of the plain of Guatemala is only 1,800 ft. above sea-level, it is perhaps higher from the level of its base than any other mountain in America. Around the base are cultivated fields, and a belt of forest and verdure extends to the top. On the summit there is a crater-like hollow measuring about 140 by 120 yards. It has received its present appellation from its occasionally emitting torrents of cold water from its northern side. On the SE is the volcanic mountain of Pacaya; and towards the W is that of Guatemala. - Juarros. - Stephens.—Thompson.
AGUA ANIMA, or Augusta, a river in the island of Cuba, which flows into Puerto Guantanamo bay, after a course of 30 m. from NW to SE.
AGUA BLANCA, a v. of Venezuela, between Araure and San Carlos, on a small stream of the same name, a branch of the Cogede.
AGUA-BRANCA, a mountain-ridge of Brazil, in the prov. of Pernambuco, 7 leagues from the Paulo Affonso fall of the San Francisco. It is covered with wild and luxuriant forests.—Henderson.

AGUACATE, or AGUA-CATTA', a range of mountains in Guatemala, rising above the town of Caldera, in the prov. of Costa Rica. It is supposed to contain immense mineral wealth; and there are several gold mines in operation upon it, the produce of which is taken to San José and Cartago, and thence to the shores of the Pacific.
AGUACATENANGO, or Concepcion Gueguet-

ENANGO, a town and district of Guatemala, in the prov. of Totonicapan. The district forms the western part of the prov. The town is in N lat. 15° 40'; 33 leagues NNW from Guatemala. 論 Die KENGA Kenner Peddan Morde Byllin Middle Gund o Kalle Caste " River Koleday Mirka B O Ku o Debilbut. V in 12 ~ Marter . . Inda . AGUACHAPAN, a town formerly of Guatemala, now of Salvador, in Central America. It is the most considerable town between Sonsonate and Guatemala, having a pop. of from 7,000 to 8,000. Earthenware is a staple manufacture of the place; and sugar is extensively cultivated in the neighbourhood. Below the table-land on which the town stands, a vast plain opens. In the neighbourhood is the beautiful lake of A., and numerous hot springs.

AGUADA, or AGUEDA, a river of Spain, in the prov. of Salamanca, which, rising in the Sierra de Gata, passes Ciudad-Rodrigo, and flows into the Douro, on the Portuguese frontier, 3 m. NW of Fregenada, after a course of 70 m. from S to N .- Also a town on the W side of Blanco or Blanquilla, one of the Leeward islands; in N lat. 11° 40', W long. 64° 42'. [De Mayne's chart.]-Also a town on the coast of Bolivia, in the prov. of Potosi.
AGUADERAS (SIERRA DE), a branch of the Sierras Alpujarras, running from W to E, through the prov. of Murcia, and terminating in Cape Palos.

AGUADILLA, a town on the NW side of the island of Porto Rico, at the bottom of a small bay with good anchorage, between Cape Aguada on the N, and Point San Francisco on the S; in N lat. 18° 26', W long. 67° 8'. Refreshments and good water may be had. [Admiralty Chart.—Raper, 3d ed.]—Also a branch of the river Chagres, which joins it near its entrance into the Caribbean sea.

AGUADOS, a name of frequent occurrence in Spanish American topography, denoting small lakes, and sometimes large ponds which fill during the rainy season, and, in numerous instances, appear to have been artificially constructed for the purpose of forming reservoirs. They are to be distinguished from the senotes, which are immense circular excavations, from 60 to 200 ft. in diameter, with rugged perpendicular sides, and having, at the depth of 50 to 100 ft. below the surface, a great body of water which always maintains nearly the same level.
AGUAGE (EL), a town of Mexico, in the provof Sonora, 75 m. NNE of Guaymas, on the road to Pětic.
AGUALCO, or TEPE AGULCO, a v. of Mexico, in the prov. of Puebla, on the road from Xalapa to Puebla.
AGUALULCO, a town of Mexico, in the prov. and 40 m. SW of Guadalaxara, in N lat. 20° 44'.
AGUAN, a river of Honduras, which divides Taguzgalpa from Honduras, and flows into the Caribbean sea 60 m. to the W of Cape Cameron. It is navigable by canoes for 40 leagues from its mouth.
AGUANUEVA, a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Chihuahua, on the road betwixt Chihuahua and Paso del Norte.
AGUAPEY. See AGOAPEHY.
AGUAR, a town of Hindostan, in Bahar, on the Dheemora river.
AGUARACATI, a lake of Buenos Ayres, in S lat. 25°.

AGUARICO, a town of Ecuador, on the road from Salvador on a head-branch of the Napo, to Puerto on a head-branch of the Putomayo. AGUARY, ARAWARY, or ARAGUARI, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, rising on the frontiers of French Guayana, flowing E, and entering the ocean by several branches. By the treaty of Amiens, this river was made the line of demarcation between Portuguese and French Guayana; the boundary being continued by a line drawn westwards to the Branco, and the navigation of the A. was to be common to both nations; but by the treaty of 1817 the Wiapoc or Oayapock, nearly 200 m. to the NW, is made the common limit.

AGUAS CALIENTES, a town of Mexico in the prov. of Guadalaxara, according to Humboldt; but in that of Zacatecas according to Ward, who says that the boundary line between the two states lies between | most southern point of Africa, 28 leagues SE of the AGU Aguas Calientes and La Villita. It is in N lat. 22°, and W long. 101° 40' [Arrowsmith's map], 102° 15' [Gregg], 100 m. NE of Guadalaxara. Pop. 20,000. It is well-built and prettily situated in a level plain, on the eastern branch of a stream of the same name, about 12 m. above its junction with the De los Conejos. Its climate is salubrious; and it is one of the handsomest and most active commercial towns in Mexico. Its principal manufacture is coarse cloth, called rebozos, which, a few years ago, consumed 5,000 arobas of wool annually. [Ward.]—The warm springs in the vicinity from which it derives its name are two in number, and about 10 yards apart; they are 33° above blood-heat; the water is beautifully clear, and appears to be impregnated with copper. Several good roads afford easy communication with the surrounding country, which is fertile, well-cultivated, and thickly peopled, producing immense crops of frijol, chile, and wheat.
AGUAVERDE, a lake of Mexico, in the prov. of Cohahuila, which discharges its waters by a river falling into the Rio Bravo del Norte, on its r. bank.
AGUAVIVA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Saragossa, 9 m. SSW of Alcaniz.
AGUDO, a town of Spain, in the prov. of La Mancha, on the road from Sacernela to Puebla de Alcocer in Estremadura.
AGUEDA DE CIMA, a v. of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 7 m. E of Aveiro.
AGUGNI, or AGOO-GNEE, a group of islands to the westward of Loo-Choo. It is composed of two principal islands, Aghee and Homar.
AGUIAR, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alentejo, 16 m. SSW of Evora.
AGUIGNAN, or AGUIJAN, one of the Ladrone islands, in the North Pacific. It lies to the SE of Tinian, in N lat. 14° 40'.
AGUILA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Valladolid, 9 m. NW of Medina de Rio.
AGUILAR-DE-LA-FRONTERA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 24 m. SSE of Cordova, on the river Cabra. Pop. 800.
AGUILAR-DEL-CAMPOS, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Palencia, 40 m. NW by N of Burgos, on the road from Santander to Palencia. Pop. 1,200. It is the Aquilaria campestris of Latin writers.

AGUILAS, a small port of Spain, in the prov. of Murcia, 30 m. WSW of Carthagena. About 4 m. to the SW is the castle of San Juan de los Ferreros; and at 5 m. E & S is the elevated promontory called La Cabezo de Cope; in N lat. 37° 24′, W long. 1° 33′. The bay of A. is sheltered from SW and W winds.— Also one of the Balearic islets, to the SE of Dadaya off the N coast of Mahon.
AGUILLONES (CAPE), a headland of Spain, to the E of Cape Ortegal; in N lat. 43° 47', W long. 7° 53'. A cluster of rocky islets lies off this point.

AGUIRRA. See AQUIRE. AGUJA (CAPE)—pronounced Aguha—a prominent point of high scarped hills, on the N coast of Colombia; in N lat. 11° 18′, W long. 74° 17′; forming the northern point of the bay of Santa Martha. An islet of the same name lies off it at the distance of one-third of a mile; but the channel betwixt them is reefy.—This cape is to be distinguished from Point AGUJA on the same coast but in N lat. 12° 23', 5 m. WSW of Point Gallinas.—There is another Punta
AGUJA, forming a double cape, on the coast of Peru, in S lat. 5° 50', W long. 81° 15'.
AGUJERO, a group of rocky islets off the S coast of Hayti, in N lat. 17° 55', W long. 71° 8'.
AGULGAOM, a town of Hindostan, in the Deccan, prov. of Aurungabad, 21 m. E of Purainda.

AGULHAS (CABO DAS), or CAPE NEEDLES, the Cape of Good Hope; in S lat. 34° 51', E long. 20° 11' [Owen], S lat. 34° 58' 30' [Tuckey]. In the Voyage of the Potomac it is placed in S lat. 34° 40', E long. 20° 20'. Keeling, approaching it from the Mauritius, describes this cape as rising "like two isles; but coming more thwart, it appeared like three islands; two bays to the N making three perspicuous points, low, and seeming round." Major Rennell says: "In passing near Cape A., I observed that the cape itself, as well as the land for many miles to the west of it, is too low to be seen at more than 6 leagues from an Indiaman's poop; and that about 9 leagues WNW true from the cape, the land rises high enough to be seen at 9 or 10 leagues. Of course this high land is set for Cape A. by those who pass too far off to see the low land." The edge of the shore is rocky to a great distance both E and W of Cape A .- An extensive bank, known as Agulhas Bank, begins in S lat. 32° 30′, E long. 29°, encircles the southern point of the continent, having its southern extremity in about S lat. 37° 30', and, turning NW, terminates near the W point of St. Helena bay. The soundings on this bank to the W of Cape A. show a muddy bottom; but to the E, it is of a sandy texture and mixed with minute shells. It is said to abound in fish, and might probably be made the seat of a valuable fishery. Its vicinity is consequently denoted by the appearances of grainpuses, seals, gannets, and various sea-birds. A strong current from the eastward follows the direction of the edge of the bank, at the distance of about 150 m. from the coast [Rennell], "increasing in velocity towards the southern pitch, where it runs at times at the rate of 160 m. in 24 hours; here it changes its direction from SW to NW, setting round the pitch directly towards the cape of Good Hope, but only with half the velocity" [Tuckey]. It proceeds along the W coast of S Africa up to the mouth of the Congo, and there prejecting shores cause it to trend westwards near the equator. Increasing in breadth and power, it now becomes known as the Equatorial stream, and under that name reaches midway between the two continents [Rennell]. Ships are frequently carried to the westward quite round the cape of Good Hope, even against the strongest NW gales, by this current; but the evil may be avoided either by pushing boldly in for the land, which may be done to within 60 or 50 fathoms of soundings; or by making a course to the southward until the return of fine weather [Owen]. It appears, however, that instead of the entire body of this stream passing over to the W, a considerable part, perhaps the greater part, returns back into the Indian ocean, merging into the easterly current that issues from the S Atlantic, and passes to the southward of the Agulhas current, and to at least 100 leagues E of the bank, occasioning great eddies and irregularities by their opposing forces [Rennell]. By our older navigators the name of this cape is frequently written AGHULAS or D'AGHULAS; recent navigators frequently write it
LAGULLAS, and sometimes St. AGUILLAS.
AGULU'H, a small town of Marocco, on a hill near the low cape of the same name, which is in N lat. 29° 49', W long. 9° 48'. The country is well-cultivated round it.
AGUNA, or AGWANA, a state on the coast of Guinea, tributary to the Fantees. It lies between the Inkran and Fantee territories, extending 20 m. from E to W. Winnebah or Simpah is the principal town.

AGUSTIN (SAN), or SAN AGUSTIN DE LAS CUEVAS, one of the most charming villages in the neighbourhood of Mexico. It is situated in the midst of handsome villas and orchards, at the foot of the mountain of Ajusco, 12 m. S of the city. On the 16th of May, being the anniversary of the feast of St. Augustin, it AHL is made the scene of a kind of carnival by the Mexican citizens, at which gambling and cock-fighting are the great amusement of all classes, and in expectation of which every room in the village is hired for months in advance.
AGUSTESHUER, a town of Travancore, 4 m. NW of Cape Comorin.
AHA'BIJ-HISAR, or AGHATSCH-HISAR, a v. of Asiatic Turkey, in Anatolia, on the l. bank of the Ryndacus, to the E of Adranós.
AHADKO'I, a v. of Asiatic Turkey, in Anatolia, 6 hours E of Ushák. There are extensive ruins here.

AHAMLISH, a p. of Ireland, on the coast of Sligo. Area 16,414 acres. Pop. 8,720, nearly all Catholics. It includes the islands of Dernish and Inismurray, and the villages of Ballintemple, Grange, and Kilkiloge.

AHANTA, a state of Western Africa, on the Gold coast, between Secondee on the E, and the Ancobra river on the W. It has nearly 40 m. of sea-coast, and extends about 30 m. into the interior, where it borders on the Wassaw district. The A. territory is considered the most fertile district on the coast. The soil is fit for the cultivation of sugar, coffee, and cotton; but the principal growth is Indian corn, yams, cassada, and plantains. The Ahantas are estimated at from 30,000 to 35,000; they are governed by local headmen or authorities; but, with the exception of those living within the Dixcove territory, consider themselves subjects of the Dutch. On the coast are the Dutch settlements of Boutray, and Acquidah, and the British settlement of Dixcove.—Parl. Report on West Coast of Africa.
AHA'R, a town of Persia, the capital of Kará-dágh, on a stream of the same name, a branch of the Araxes, 82 m. by road WNW of Ardabil. It contains about 700 houses.
AHARA, a p. in co. Longford, Ireland. Area 2,595 acres. Pop. 1,036, nearly all Catholics.
AHARNEY, a parish in Queen's co., Ireland. Area 6,940 acres. Pop. 2,586, nearly all Catholics.

AHASCRAGH, a p. in co. Galway, Ireland. Area 11,026 acres. Pop. 5,380 nearly all Catholics.—The neat town of A. is on the l. bank of a stream of the same name, a branch of the Suck, 64 m. NNW of Ballinasloe.
AHAUS. See AHUUS.
AHAUSEN, a town of Hanover, in the prov. of Verden, between Rotenburg and Bremen.
AHEDO, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 13 m. NE of Burgos.
AHERLOW, a small river of Ireland, a branch of the Suir, in co. Limerick. It has a course of about 18 m., of which that part called the glen of A. is remarkable for scenic beauty.
